data.title: Stock Market Down
data.description:
Well, the "war" in Iran is doing something that little else has been able to do over the past four years.

The "war" in Iran is apparently causing a decline in the U.S. stock market.

The "war" began on Saturday, February 28, 2026.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at 48,978 the day before, February 27.

This value was slightly below the historical high for that index, a high of 50,188, which was achieved on February 10, 2026.

The S&P 500 stock index closed at 6,879 the day before, February 27.

This value was slightly below the historic high for that index, a high of 6,779, which was achieved on January 27, 2026.

Both indexes were within striking distance of their historic highs on the day before the "war" began.

Now, the DJIA closed at 46,558 on Friday, January 13, down 3,530 points from the February 27 closing.

And, the S&P 500 closed at 6,632 on Friday, January 13, down 247 points from the February 27 closing.

Here is what the picture looks like.

For the Dow Jones Industrial Average we see....

And, for the Standard & Poor's 500 Index....

What is different?

Well, monetary policy is different.

For the past four years or so, the Federal Reserve has been working to keep the economy growing and to keep inflation falling with unemployment staying around 4.0 percent.

The Fed was doing a pretty good job!

In early December 2025, the Fed decided to alter its policy by moving from a program of quantitative tightening to one of quantitative easing.

But it was still operating under the guidelines set up by Ben Bernanke when he was the Chairman of the Board of Governors toward the end of the Great Recession.

Things changed on February 28...the "war" started.

And, with the start of the "war," the way the government was spending money changed. The budget support of the "war" became the number one priority of the federal government.

And, the way the budget was financed rose to a high position of the federal government.

I wrote in my last post that

"The Federal Reserve injected substantial liquidity into the banking system following the U.S. attack on Iran, with reserve balances in the commercial banking system rising $59.5 billion in one week."

In other words, the "war" was taking on the number one focus of government policy making, and in this scenario, the Federal Reserve seemed to be providing the "banking system" with sufficient liquidity to support the funding of this new government focus.

"Quantitative easing" seems to be taking a "back seat."

And, the feeling is, that as long as the "war" effort goes on, the financing of the "war" will take precedence as far as government policy making goes.

The market response?

Well, it looks as if the response of market participants is not positive.

The Fed's monetary policy of recent years cemented the approval of the market, and market participants joined in on the effort and supported what the Federal Reserve was doing...and the stock market rose and rose and rose.

Now, uncertainty hangs over the market.

President Trump has gotten the attention of traders.

Quantitative easing or quantitative tightening is no longer "the game" that the monetary authorities are playing.

Trump wanted to control and dominate monetary policy.

Up to this point in time, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell would not let that happen.

Mr. Powell's term as Fed Chair goes into May 2026.

Then a new chairman must be approved. Only then might the focus of monetary policy change.

Now, Trump does not have to wait.

Here we see, from the Fed's balance sheet, the way "Reserve Balancers with Federal Reserve Banks" has taken off.

The first few weeks in this chart take place before "the war" started.

The gains had been modest and consistent with the period of quantitative easing that the Federal Reserve had been operating in since the start of December 2025.

As I reported in my last post, reserve balances with Federal Reserve banks rose by about $63.0 billion in the last two weeks.

This is not just a minor amount!

And, we now have to see what the Federal Reserve does going forward.

So, it looks as if we have "a new world" to contemplate.

And, it is a world more like the one Trump wanted to be working in and less like the world that Fed Chair Jerome Powell had been operating in.

The stock markets did not like this.

Stock prices fell.

And, they are likely to fall further in the future.

One reason for stocks to fall in the future is that the world has changed, and market participants must absorb what Trump and his team are doing and what this means for the future.

For one thing, the future will be a lot less certain.

The current approach to monetary policy has been in place since, at least, the fall of 2009.

As mentioned above, market participants had gotten quite used to the way the Fed was operating and came to have quite a bit of "trust" in what the Fed was trying to do.

Now, these market participants don't know what the federal government is going to be doing with respect to the "war" situation, and they will have little or no idea about how the Federal Reserve is going to support the "war" effort.

Plus, the Federal Reserve will have a new Chairman in the near future. How the new chairman will work out is anybody's guess. He will be new at the job!

This will mean that Trump will have most of the "cards" to play going forward.

And, this is just what Trump wants!

The stock market has declined over the past two weeks.

The stock market will, in all likelihood, continue to decline and stay weak into the coming months. The environment has shifted.

Stay tuned for a volatile ride!

data.title: Carnival: Why I'm Doubling Down Despite Unhedged Fuel Risk
data.description:
Carnival Corporation (CCL) stock has been in freefall since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran a couple of weeks ago. From around $32, the stock has fallen to less than $24 as of this writing, representing a sharp decline of 25%. This market selloff has erased almost all gains since my previous article on Carnival, which was published in May 2025. Back then, I thought Carnival's fleet modernization efforts were underappreciated in the market. I pointed to strong revenue growth and a meaningful improvement in ROIC as a result of these investments. These expectations materialized in H2 2025 with Carnival reaching record profitability of $3.1 billion for FY 2025. Revenue also hit a record high of $26.6 billion. Balance sheet improvements were noteworthy too. After years of struggling with pandemic-related debt, Carnival's net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio improved to 3.4x in 2025. From a high of $32 billion in 2022, Carnival's long-term debt balance has declined to a more reasonable $24 billion thanks to aggressive debt repayments. All these improvements paved the way for Carnival to reinstate its dividend as well.

Everything was going well for Carnival until two weeks ago.

Rising Middle East tensions present two different challenges for Carnival.

  1. Carnival does not hedge its exposure to energy prices, unlike some of its closest rivals. Energy prices have shot through the roof in March.
  2. The rosy demand outlook painted by the management during the last earnings release may not hold true anymore. Itinerary changes are already happening.

When it comes to demand challenges, Carnival can avoid the Middle East and redirect the demand to some of its other popular destinations. I'm not saying that Carnival will not feel the impact of a demand shock in the Middle East, but this is comparatively more manageable than fuel cost inflation.

Therefore, in this analysis, I will try to quantify the impact of rising fuel prices on Carnival's profitability in 2026 to determine if the market is correct to punish CCL harshly.

Quantifying Carnival's Exposure To Rising Energy Costs

Since the start of the attacks on Iran, crude oil prices have been very volatile. After trading for around $60/barrel, WTI crude prices shot through the roof to reach $100 soon after Iran claimed the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. As of this writing, WTI crude is priced around $94/barrel. If you're a Carnival investor, looking at WTI prices only reveals half the truth. This is because Carnival uses different types of fuels to power its cruise ships. Some of the main types of energy sources used by Carnival include:

  1. Heavy fuel oil
  2. LNG
  3. Very low sulfur fuel oil
  4. MGO

Based on company filings, the absolute majority of Carnival's ships use HFO. HFO is cheaper to burn compared to MGO, and Carnival has made strong efforts to transition its fleet into energy-efficient vessels by focusing on alternative energy sources. The company also uses VLSFO, which is again cheaper to burn than MGO. As illustrated below, VLFSO prices have surged to almost $1,000/metric ton from around $600/metric ton before Iran attacks.

Exhibit 1: VLSFO prices

When MGO prices averaged around $800/metric ton in late 2025, Carnival guided for a blended average fuel cost of $524/metric ton for 2026. For Q1 2026, the company expects fuel costs of $539/metric ton. These expectations reveal the positive impact on energy costs stemming from Carnival's efforts to embrace lower-cost energy sources. Today, MGO prices have eclipsed $1,350/metric ton after surging by more than $500/metric ton since the beginning of the month.

To quantify the potential impact of increased energy prices, we need to first look at Carival's fuel consumption expectations. According to the company, Carnival will utilize 2.8 metric tons of fuel in 2026 at an average fuel cost of $524/ton.

Exhibit 2: Carnival fuel consumption guidance for 2026

In a separate segment in the 10-Q filing, the management notes that a 10% change in its blended fuel cost will have a $145 million impact on net income. This makes our calculations quite easy.

Given that Carnival's expected adjusted net income of $3.45 billion for 2026 is likely to be impacted by rising fuel costs, we need to assign certain probabilities to the outcome of the Iran war to determine by how much to reduce net income expectations.

Given that Middle East tensions seem likely to drag on for some time (I am no political expert, but I expect these tensions to have an impact on energy prices at least through the summer), I am modeling a 20% increase in Carnival's blended average fuel cost for 2026 to $629/metric ton. Applying the management's sensitivity framework, we can arrive at a $290 million impact on net income for 2026 in this scenario. This would reduce the adjusted income expectation to $3.16 billion.

As a word of caution, this impact is calculated assuming net yields will remain the same. The impact of rising fuel costs may be mitigated by an increase in net yields. To be on the conservative side, however, I am modeling net yields to meet the management guidance.

Reasons To Be Contrarian

I have discussed my investment thesis for Carnival in detail in my previous analyses. Today, after a flash decline in its stock price, I can think of a few reasons to be contrarian and double down on CCL stock.

First, the company is cheaply valued at a forward P/E of just 9.5x. Even after modeling a sizeable 20% hit to its blended average fuel cost this year, Carnival's adjusted net income is expected to be over $3.1 billion in 2026. This would still mark a YoY increase in net income, albeit a small one. I believe Mr. Market is overlooking the company's solid earnings power due to short-term fluctuations in fuel prices. Now that the worst has already been priced into CCL stock, I believe the chances of a positive surprise are significantly higher than any more negative surprises to oil prices.

Second, Carnival has successfully deleveraged its balance sheet after several years of strategic efforts to aggressively repay debt and refinance at better rates. This is a structural, fundamental, permanent improvement in its business. Unlike volatile oil prices, this structural improvement will reward the company for years. I believe the market is currently overlooking these improvements in a big way just because the company was forced to accumulate debt at a record pace to survive during the pandemic. As I wrote several years ago on this platform, Carnival had no other choice but to see its balance sheet health deteriorate as it fought to survive during pandemic days.

Third, Carnival enjoys pricing power behind the curtains, which is something the market has been oblivious to for quite some time now. If you look at 2026 booking data, even at historically high prices, more than two-thirds of Carnival's capacity is already booked. As confirmed by the company, there are no new ship deliveries scheduled for 2026 (see chart below). This means that supply is scarce while demand is at near record levels. Carnival is enjoying pricing power today, but the market is still turning a blind eye to this.

Exhibit 3: Ship deliveries

The dividend reinstatement is also a positive development, but not necessarily a catalyst in my eyes given that I am a long-term investor with a primary focus on market-beating capital appreciation, opposed to current period income.

Carnival Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Preview

Carnival is expected to report Q1 FY 2026 earnings on March 20. The company has guided for adjusted net income of $235 million and adjusted EBITDA of $1.24 billion for the quarter. Net yields are expected to grow 1.6% YoY. Although this may sound negligible, we need to remember that Carnival is facing tough comps this quarter as it had a strong Q1 2025.

More than the headline numbers, I believe Carnival management's commentary on the impact of Middle East tensions on its itineraries and fuel costs will have a bigger role to play in the post-earnings trading session. I am keeping an eye on a few areas here.

One such area is management commentary on how they plan to redeploy its ships. Investors will have to account for any discounts the company may offer due to changed itineraries as such discounts will impact the company's yields. Carnival will have to strike a balance between preserving occupancy rates while maximizing revenue, so this is going to be an insightful earnings call that will reveal the management's strategy to navigate the challenging times.

Another obvious area is the management's commentary on fuel prices. Not just prices, I will listen for clues on the availability of certain types of fuel as well.

I will also look for clues to gauge a measure of how Middle East tensions have impacted - or at least expected to impact - demand for cruises going forward.

Risks

One thing that I want to always avoid is betting on the outcome of a geopolitical development. Things might actually get worse from here, in which case the 20% increase in Carnival's blended average fuel cost I modeled for 2026 could prove to be too low. That would drag adjusted net income well below what the company reported for FY 2025. I believe the market will punish CCL if this happens.

From a long-term perspective, we need to keep an eye on the risks arising from Carnival's fleet modernization efforts as well. Although the benefits far outweigh the risks under normal circumstances, if demand deteriorates sharply in the next few years as a result of unsettled Middle East tensions, the company's operating efficiency will take a big hit.

Takeaway

Carnival is too cheap to ignore. Although the company's cost base will be materially impacted by rising fuel prices, I believe investors should look beyond this short-term volatility in energy prices to appreciate the promising long-term growth outlook for the company. In any case, I believe Carnival should not have been valued as cheaply as it was before the Iran crisis. The tensions have only pushed the company into deeply undervalued territory. I have decided to add more CCL shares to my portfolio ahead of the earnings report, not to profit from an uptick in the stock price in the post-earnings trading session but because I believe Carnival is very attractively priced today.

data.title: Tsakos Energy Navigation: Getting Cautious On The Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

In a previous article on Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN), I argued investors didn’t have to choose between the common shares and the preferred shares. The preferred shares offered an interesting income component, but the strengthening tanker market would boost the earnings and cash flows attributable to the common shareholders.

Good results in 2025, with more to come in 2026

The final quarter of 2025 was a good quarter for Tsakos, as the company was able to combine a higher revenue with lower operating expenses compared to the fourth quarter of 2024. As you can see below, the total revenue increased by approximately $34M while the total expenses decreased by roughly $5M on the back of lower voyage expenses and G&A expenses, partially offset by higher vessel operating expenses.

But the impact on the operating income was clear: the operating income almost doubled to just over $81M, while the slightly higher net interest expenses weren’t sufficient to have a noteworthy impact on the net income, which came in at $58.9M. After also deducting the net income attributable to the non-controlling interests as well as the $6.75M in quarterly preferred share dividends, the net income attributable to the common shareholders of Tsakos was approximately $50.7M for an EPS of $1.70.

And if you’d take a step back and look at the full-year result, you’ll see that although the FY 2025 result was a bit lighter than the FY 2024 result, the EPS of $4.45 was very robust, especially considering there was a $36M additional gain on the sale of vessels in FY 2024, which represented in excess of $1/share. So if you’d only look at the actual financial performance of the operating entity (and exclude non-recurring items like the gain or loss on the sale of vessels), Tsakos had an excellent 2025, mainly thanks to a very strong final quarter of the year.

Preferred dividends obviously remain well-covered, and with $1.86B in total equity on the balance sheet, I like the asset coverage ratio for the preferred shares as well. There’s just under $300M in preferred equity outstanding, which means the ‘cushion’ of common equity that should absorb first losses has increased to almost $1.6B. Which emphasizes the superior position of the preferred stock.

Meanwhile, the common shares will benefit from the current tailwinds. An older VLCC was sold, 22 of its vessels that are employed in the spot market, and the locked-in contract revenue now exceeds $4B. This should set Tsakos up for a strong 2026.

The preferred shares remain attractive, but call dates are coming closer

I have a long position in the Series F preferred shares (TEN.PR.F) that were issued by Tsakos Energy Navigation. That series of preferred stock is a so-called fixed-to-float preferred share, and from July 2028 on, the preferred dividend gets updated to a floating-rate dividend based on the 3-month benchmark interest rate. Originally, the 3M LIBOR, we can now assume future floating dividends will be based on the SOFR. That being said, I expect the preferred shares to be called, as Tsakos’ management has indicated on several occasions that it would like to do so (this was reiterated on the Q4 2025 call, when the management indicated it ‘may be looking at repurchasing preferreds’ next year, when the Series E preferred stock can be called). A lot can happen between now and the summer of 2028, but I am operating under the assumption that the Series F preferred shares will get called in July 2028.

This also means that I should look at the Series F preferred stock as a 28-month term investment. Anyone who buys the stock now will still receive 10 quarterly preferred dividends of $0.59375 each, resulting in a yield to call of approximately 5%.

While that still represents a mark-up of approximately 130 bps to the current 2 year US Treasury yield, the appeal of the Series F preferred shares is decreasing given the current premium of in excess of 10% to the call price. If Tsakos indeed decides to retire this series of preferred capital in July 2028, there will be a certain 10% capital loss versus today’s share price.

Investment thesis

Despite that, the preferred shares are still a ‘buy’ (although a very light buy) for investors looking to invest cash with a 2-2.5 year investment horizon. While the markup to the 2-year US Treasury yield is pretty low, Tsakos Energy’s balance sheet has improved fast; the company continues to rejuvenate its fleet while ensuring it’s still running a liquid balance sheet. And with in excess of $1.5B in common equity on the balance sheet, there is a pretty good cushion available for the preferred shareholders in case something does go wrong.

I also have a long position in the common shares issued by Tsakos, and I’m not interested in selling just yet (despite the strong share price performance). The company is taking advantage of the current strong tailwinds in the sector, which may result in a surprisingly strong 2026.

data.title: FOMC Meeting Takes Center Stage; Micron, Alibaba To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major indexes moved lower on Friday as investors digested a fresh batch of economic data, highlighted by the latest PCE inflation and GDP reports. On the economic front, core PCE inflation rose 3.1% Y/Y in January, a touch hotter than expected. Furthermore, the U.S. GDP growth estimate was revised lower to +0.7% in Q4.

As the earnings season fades, the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision is expected to take center stage next week. It will be out on Wednesday along with February's PPI and crude oil inventory numbers. The FOMC will hold its press conference on Thursday. Initial jobless claims and new home sales data for March are also scheduled for the day. Fed's chair Jerome Powell is scheduled to speak on Saturday.

Micron (MU), Tencent (TCEHY), and Alibaba (BABA) are among the companies reporting their results next week.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday: Dollar Tree (DLTR). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: Lululemon (LULU). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: Tencent, Micron. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Alibaba, FedEx (FDX). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday: XPeng (XPEV). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) and AXT (AXTI) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Itafos (ITFS), Alto Ingredients (ALTO), and Cabral Gold (CBGZF). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Cion Investment (CION), Sotera (SHC), and eXp World Holdings (EXPI). Short interest is elevated on Better Home & Finance (BETR) and Lucid Group (LCID) following its Investor Day event.

IPO watch: No new IPOs are expected to begin trading. The IPO lockup expires on certain blocks of shares of Netskope (NTSK) and Pattern Group (PTRN). The quiet period expires on Sharon AI (SHAZ) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include AIG (AIG), Meta Platforms (META), Merck (MRK), and Kohl's (KSS).

Micron Earnings Preview: Micron is seen posting second-quarter earnings of $8.66 per share, while revenue is projected to see a multi-fold jump to $19.18B. Analysts are optimistic ahead of the earnings, expecting the firm to beat consensus, benefiting from soaring DRAM prices and operating leverage. The management's third-quarter guidance will be pivotal for investors. "…while memory pricing tailwinds persist, industry cyclicality and potential overinvestment could temper future growth," Seeking Alpha analyst Jonathan Weber said. However, beyond this year, Micron's earnings growth is projected to slow, with EPS expected to climb roughly 35% next year before a modest decline the year after, the analyst noted.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oracle's Debt-Ridden AI Ambitions Are Cheaply Valued - Maintain Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Oracle Corporation (ORCL) in December 2025, discussing why I had upgraded the stock to a buy then, thanks to the robust cloud-related growth prospects, the discounted valuations compared to its hyperscaler peers thanks to the prior correction, the consequently rich capital appreciation prospects, and the ongoing bottoming as observed in the oversold technical indicators.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the ORCL stock here, thanks to the improved risk/reward after the materialization of a trading floor at $136s after the prior AI/SaaSpocalypse meltdown.

This is on top of their aggressive cloud expansion already driving accelerated SaaS monetization opportunities and the potential market share gains against the Big Three's current dominance, despite the intermediate-term balance sheet and adj. EPS risks.

ORCL's Debt-Ridden AI Ambitions

Since my last Buy rating, ORCL has further retraced by double digits to bottom at the $136s by February 05, 2026, thanks to the triple whammy of cooling AI trade, AI debt fears, and SaaSpocalypse fears, as the market notably discounts the former's data center and SaaS capabilities.

The same has been observed in ORCL's steep selloff by -55.1% since the 52 weeks high - an unsurprising development, since ORCL has emerged as the "poster child of the shift from mostly equity-funded AI spending to debt."

The same has been similarly observed in them "adding debt via off-balance sheet deals. Its most recent 10-Q quarterly financial statement showed a quarter-trillion dollars of lease-payment commitments, substantially all of which are related to data centers and cloud capacity."

Therefore, while the recent Q4'25 earnings season has reiterated the insatiable compute demand and the necessarily growing data center-related spending trends entering 2026, I can understand why the market has opted to sell first and ask questions later.

This is despite the market analysts' projections of the global data center capacity expected to "nearly double from 103 GW to 200 GW by 2030," as the market seemingly focuses on the sustainability of the hyperscalers' aggressive capex trends against their balance sheet health and bottom-line prospects.

For example, thanks to ORCL's outsized YTD capex of $39.17B (+222.9% YoY) against the lower YTD net cash provided by operating activities at $17.35B (+18.3% YoY), it is unsurprising that the company has reported a negative YTD Free Cash Flow [FCF] generation of $21.82B (-962% YoY).

This reason is also why ORCL has reported an inflated net debt of $95.47B in FQ3'26 (-17.3% YoY), compared to its direct hyperscaler peers, including:

  • Amazon (AMZN) at FCF generation of $11.19B in FY2025 (-70.7% YoY) and net cash of $57.38B (+18.1% YoY),
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) at FCF generation of $73.26B in FY2025 (inline YoY) and net cash of $80.29B (-5.2% YoY), and
  • Microsoft (MSFT) at FCF generation of $31.54B on a YTD basis (+21.3% YoY) and net cash of $49.2B (+54.5% YoY), respectively,

all of which directly explain ORCL's impacted adjusted EPS performance, as observed in the former's TTM interest coverage ratio of 4.98x, compared to AMZN at 35.2x, GOOG at 176.7x, and MSFT at 53.3x.

These reasons are also why I am encouraged by the seemingly moderated data center capex trends, as observed in ORCL and OpenAI (OPENAI) "decided not to expand their data center" lease in Abilene, Texas, which is likely to temper some of the balance sheet and bottom-line risks in the near term.

Otherwise, with ORCL still expecting to spend $50B on capex in FY2026 (+135.7% YoY) after reporting $21.21B in FY2025 (+209.1% YoY), I am of the opinion that the hyperscaler is likely to continue reporting impacted FCF ahead, pending further clarity on their FY2027 capex plans in the upcoming FQ4'26 earnings call sometime in June 2026.

ORCL's Capacity Expansions Drive SaaS Growth

Otherwise, ORCL appears to be well positioned to deliver their reiterated FY2026 revenue guidance of $67B (+16.7% YoY), based on the robust YTD revenue growth profile of +16.1% YoY.

Combined with the ongoing buildout of their new data centers, it is unsurprising that the management has felt confident to raise their FY2027 revenue guidance to $90B (+34.3% YoY), with it underscoring how the intensified capex has been accretive to their top-line growth prospects despite the prolonged time-lag to revenue recognition.

If anything, ORCL's promising FY2027 guidance has been well supported by the outsized, multi-year Remaining Performance Obligations of $553B (+325% YoY), with it presenting investors with a great insight into its future top/bottom-line performance.

Combined with the improved cross-selling trends across its IaaS and SaaS offerings, as observed in the growing Multicloud Database revenues by +531% YoY, it is apparent that the management's decision to open up their database platform to all clouds has been instrumental in driving their profitable, recurring SaaS revenue cadence.

This is on top of their growing AI infrastructure revenue by +243% YoY, as demand for compute capacity remains gated by supply, with their AI infrastructure gross margin likely to continue expanding from the 32% reported in FQ3'26 (within their target range of 30% to 40%), once more of their under-construction capacity is completed:

And so as our business is going through this hypergrowth phase, that's the only drag on profitability. But thankfully, we're getting -- we're very good in getting better at delivering that capacity. That capacity, when we deliver it, is all already contracted for at a very profitable rate.

So when you combine those things together, we're extremely confident in both the capacity we delivered and the -- continuing to increase profitability of our AI business.

(Seeking Alpha)

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain rather promising, with ORCL still expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +31%/+21.1% through FY2028, respectively.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +8%/+9.4% and a 10Y CAGR of +4.1%/+8.1%, respectively, with it apparent that their aggressive data center build out and the consequently, higher compute capacity have been able to drive renewed SaaS growth prospects.

While ORCL has yet to offer their long-term capex guidance, I am of the opinion that the intensified compute expansions are likely to remain elevated in the intermediate term, with the stock only suitable for those comfortable with elevated debt risks despite the management introducing new financing models:

Through our partners, we have secured more than 10 gigawatts of power and data center capacity coming online over the next 3 years.

We also continue to innovate on our business models. On our last earnings call, I shared multiple ideas for how we can incrementally grow our AI infrastructure without Oracle raising more debt or issuing equity... A combination of bring your own hardware and upfront customer payments enables us to continue expanding without any negative cash flow from Oracle.

(Seeking Alpha)

ORCL's Debt Risks Are Already Priced In

Thanks to the -55.1% meltdown from the 52-week highs, it is unsurprising that ORCL is a lot more compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 8.73x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.38x, down from the recent highs of 14.82x/46.72x while nearing the 5-year means of 7.80x/21.85x, respectively.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.38x and the consensus adj. EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of 21.1%, it is apparent that ORCL is compellingly valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.01x, compared to the 5Y mean of 1.90x.

When comparing ORCL's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.38x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.01x to its diversified peers, including:

  • hyperscaler - AMZN at 27.14x/1.53x (using the same methodology),
  • hyperscaler - GOOG at 26.25x/1.89x,
  • hyperscaler/enterprise SaaS - MSFT at 24.02x/1.34x,
  • enterprise analytics SaaS - Palantir (PLTR) at 116.13x/2.22x,
  • enterprise creative SaaS - Adobe (ADBE) at 11.48x/0.99x, and
  • enterprise analytics/workflow SaaS - Snowflake (SNOW) at 99.18x/2.52x, respectively,

it is apparent that ORCL's profitable growth prospects are cheaply valued after the prior SaaSpocalypse/AI meltdown, as their cloud segments also comprise a larger part of their growth story.

Otherwise, it is also important to note that ORCL's discounted P/E valuations may be attributed to their overly done data center spending trends, and the consequently impacted adj. EPS prospects are attributed to the elevated interest expenses, as discussed above.

These developments underscore the need for investors to closely monitor ORCL's capex buildout and the consequent effect on their bottom-lines, since they are likely to trigger near-term noise on their performance metrics.

ORCL Is An Even Better Buy Here

For now, the bulls have defended the new uptrend support line, as observed in ORCL's bounce at the $136s in February 2026, with it implying the end of the prior SaaS/AI pessimism after the breach against the prior uptrend support line since late 2022.

A similar conclusion may also be derived through ORCL's recovering technical indicators across the RSIs and trading volumes, with a bottom already materializing by February 2026 and the next recovery leg likely to occur upon the materialization of a positive catalyst.

This is especially since the stock is now trading near to my fair value estimates of $154.30, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.38x (near to its 5Y mean of 21.85x and cheap against the hyperscaler peer group mean of 24x) and the LTM adj EPS performance of $7.22 (+21.1% sequentially).

Based on the consensus adj EPS estimates of $10.72, there remains an excellent upside potential of +47.7% to my long-term price target of $229.10 as well, with it apparent that the recent moderation in Q2'25/H2'25 euphoria has been a boon for those looking to add.

As a result of their aggressive cloud expansion already driving accelerated SaaS monetization opportunities, the potential market share gains (currently at 3%, inline QoQ/YoY) against the Big Three's current dominance, the cheaper valuations from the recent meltdown, and the improved risk/reward after the materialization of a trading floor at $136s, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the ORCL stock here, despite the intermediate-term balance sheet and adj EPS risks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Procter & Gamble Company: Dividend Intact Amid Ongoing Restructuring
data.description:
Setting The Stage

In the midst of uncertainty in the macroeconomic environment marked by persistent inflation, heightening household debt, and softening consumer demand, lies The Procter & Gamble Company (PG). I am seeing this company as a resilient player in this turbulent market. Its moat lies within its consumer staples business. The company has about 65 brands sold in close to 70 countries globally. Its brands target essential categories that people must buy regardless of the economic situation. This includes detergents, diapers, shampoo, and toothpaste. I am simply describing PG as a defensive stock.

Having said that, I am alive to the fact that being defensive alone doesn’t warrant a buy rating. The biggest question that investors should be asking themselves is whether or not the current valuation coupled with restructuring and the current organic growth trajectory offers a desirable risk-reward tradeoff to warrant buying this stock. From where I stand, I believe it does. For this reason, I am rating it a buy.

Recent Financial Performance: A Tale Of Flat Organic But Steady Cash

PG reported its Q2 2026 on January 22nd, a quarter characterized by mixed performance. To begin with, its net sales of $22.2 billion grew by about 1.5% YoY, although the headline organic sales growth was flat. This was a drop from 3% organic growth in the previous year. The dip was largely attributed to a volume decline of about 1%.

From the profitability side, gross margin contracted by 50 bps with tariff-related costs alone accounting for 60 bps points of this headwind. However, total productivity savings of about 270 bps partially offset this setback. Core EPS came in at $1.88 marking a flat YoY growth, with GAAP diluted EPS at $1.78 down 5% due to restructuring charges.

Other than the muted organic growth, the other major highlight of the quarter was the cash side of the narrative. The company reported an operating cash flow of $5 billion and adjusted free cash flow productivity improvement to 88% from 84% in the previous year.

Notably, PG returned $4.8 billion to shareholders, $2.5 billion as dividends and $2.3 billion in buybacks. This, in my view, underscores the company’s reliability in converting earnings into cash and distributing consistently. I said consistently deliberately, given that this was the company’s 69th consecutive year of dividend payment.

Given this brief overview of the most recent quarterly performance, it's apparent that the company is feeling the heat of the tough macroeconomic environment. The flat organic growth underscores a weakening consumer demand, and the margin compressions signal the effects of higher tariffs on profitability. Nevertheless, the company’s resilience is underpinned by its solid cash flow performance. Either way, I believe the most important thing for now isn’t the current performance, but rather, the ongoing restructuring.

Is Structural Pivoting A Response To Weak Organic Growth?

When I look at this company holistically, I don’t think that organic growth rate is the most important aspect to focus on at the moment. Instead, I think its operational restructuring is, because growth would be a consequence of it. Let me summarize the scope in case you missed it.

One thing that I would like to highlight here is the company’s clear position that the restructuring savings are being reinvested into growth and not being used to solidify short-term EPS. I am pleased by this move because it is forward-looking even though it pressures near-term margins. The company is simply trading short-term earnings and a smooth curve for a structurally better cost base and more focused portfolio. It’s a big call for sure! Secondly, I find the rationale of the portfolio optimization very sound. By exiting markets where returns are thin, PG is focusing its firepower on higher-margin segments. If you look at the total productivity savings growth of more than 270 bps by Q2 2026, this serves as the early positive signs of the correct path this company is taking.

Organic Sales Growth Conundrum: Volume Recovery As The Key Variable

You will agree with me that price-led organic growth, although valuable, has a limit. Although real wages seem to be growing faster in what seems like a positive indicator for this company, inflation is persistently high above 3%, and it is estimated to remain above that range in 2026.

Imagining this with what is an already weak consumer purchasing power, it tells you that a price hike is not an option, and should it be, it could be detrimental. The obvious conclusion is that, volume growth is the most important metric for PG this year. In Q2, the company’s volume decline of 1% was majorly attributed to demand collapse due to residual effects of several years of compounded pricing.

With this in mind, recall that the company’s FY2026 guidance calls for organic sales growth of about 0%-4%. Having registered 2% growth in Q1 and 0% in Q2, it follows that in H2 2026 the company must deliver close to 2%-4% to hit even the midpoint of their target range. While this is possible, especially given the company’s product pipeline, such as the launches of new products, it isn’t a guarantee if the company keeps hiking prices. For sustainable growth to be realized, volumes must improve, and this must come alongside stable prices. I think this explains the company’s recent pricing strategy shift, which saw a reduction in some major products' prices. This new playbook could prove instrumental in restoring volume uptrend and hence sustainable organic growth.

My Take On The Dividends

In my opening paragraphs I mentioned that PG has a streak of 69 years of dividend payments and, even better, a similar trajectory of dividend growth. This is stellar compared to the sector medians, which are far behind. To say the least, PG remains in a category of one!

With a quarterly dividend of $1.06/share annualized to $4.23 this company’s dividend is quite sustainable. I am saying so looking at its operating cash flow, which has been on a dominant uptrend over the years. For more than 10 years now, the company has sustained its trailing CFO above $12 billion, a testament to its strong dividend foundation.

At a ~68% payout ratio and assuming the management’s dividend target of $10 billion in dividend payments in FY2026, I don’t see any risk in this dividend policy. Even if organic sales growth remains flat, I believe Q2 was a testament that PG can generate sufficient cash to maintain and grow its payout. Indeed, its historical dividend trend and CFO lend credence to this assertion.

Valuation: A Rare Discount To Historical Norm

To most investors, valuation is where the bull case finds its rationale or, rather, becomes more tangible. As of 14th March 2026, PG is trading at about 21.6x forward PE, quite a discount to its 5-year average of 24.18x. In my view, for a company with PG’s cash flow reliability and dividend track record, I think any discount to long run averages is worth taking.

A dividend yield of 2.81% above the company’s average of 2.45% signals that the market is pricing in more uncertainty than usual. For income investors, this is a buying opportunity rather than a warning sign. PG’s dividend is exceptionally well covered at a 67.88% payout supported by solid operating cash flow.

Risks

While I am rating this stock a buy, it is not without a risk. The tariffs imposed under the US trade policy represent a material and concrete headwind. PG projects approximately $400 million in after-tax tariff-related costs for FY 2026 and about $100 million in commodity cost challenges. In Q2 2026 alone, tariffs accounted for 60 bps of gross margin compression. This serves as a major threat to the company’s profitability, and investors should keep a close eye on how the situation evolves.

The Bottom Line

May I note that PG is not a high-growth story and should not be evaluated as one? The appropriate lens to look through is a high quality, compounding dividend payer with a solid cash flow backing. At the current valuation, investors are getting a 69-year streak of dividend payment and growth at a discount. For me, this is an outright buy for income dividend-oriented investors.

data.title: PFXF Challenges The S&P 500's Earnings Yield <4% With A Yield >6%
data.description:
I have always appreciated preferred stocks. Although very criticized for their inability to generate excess value compared to the equity segment, I have personally always seen them as an equity-like asset with a competitive distribution for an income-oriented portfolio. And it may just be a personal opinion, but among these, I have always appreciated the fact that VanEck Preferred Securities ex Financials ETF (PFXF) approaches this segment by excluding the banking sector. Evidence that since 2023 seems to have brought added value compared to competitors and a distribution that has generated a lot of discussion. Today, with the conflict in Iran, higher rates for longer, and an S&P 500 earnings yield, what value can an instrument like PFXF have in a portfolio? Well, it depends. On what? Also on how wide the credit spread is. But first …

What is PFXF really?

It is an ETF that invests in hybrid debt, preferred stock, and convertible preferred stock issued by non-financial companies. Specifically, this vehicle provides exposure to non-financial corporate hybrid capital, meaning instruments that sit between equity and subordinated debt. The benchmark indicated as the current underlying is the ICE Exchange-Listed Fixed & Adjustable Rate Non-Financial Preferred Securities Index, although before June 1, 2021, the reference was the Wells Fargo Hybrid and Preferred Securities ex Financials Index.

It has an expense ratio of 0.40%: a cost that is higher than the expense range that would normally be preferred for long-term passive exposures, but consistent when read in light of an underlying market that is difficult to replicate efficiently security by security.

A cost, in fact, well covered by the yield of preferreds. The ETF distributes income monthly and can distribute annual capital gains. In fact, it has a trailing dividend yield around 6.78% and a 30-day SEC yield of 6.46%.

The strategy

The strategy that emerges is to offer exposure to preferred securities, hybrid debt, and convertible preferred of non-financial issuers. And this is a differentiating element: in my view, this makes PFXF a form of exposure to subordinated corporate/hybrid capital of utilities, REITs, telecom, industrials, and other capital-intensive sectors that is difficult to find in the market. This is because it presents itself as a passive but still selective management: it concentrates risk on a relatively small investable market, on specific issuers, and on instruments often with very long duration. In this sense, the strategy will be more sensitive to interest rate risk, corporate credit risk, and infrastructure/real estate sector risk.

How it is built (holding distribution)

The portfolio contains just over one hundred instruments (95.22% USA), but this does not automatically equate to true diversification, because the fund is non-diversified and some companies appear with multiple different issues. The fund then fragments if we look at the category of the individual instruments, in my opinion roughly summarizable like this:

If we then look at maturities, the diversification by maturity can, in my view, be summarized as in this table, which essentially makes PFXF a vehicle with very high duration, therefore closely linked to movements in long-term yields.

Added to this is the fact that more than half of the portfolio is unrated, the bulk of the rated component is concentrated in BBB, and the aggregate credit quality has been interpreted as something close to BBB- / BB+.

From the official fact sheet, it emerges that the top 10 include Boeing at 9.47%, Albemarle at 4.74%, Strategy at 4.06%, NextEra in two lines, Southern Company, Microchip, HPE, PG&E, and AT&T, with the top 10 totaling around 36%. For an ETF with over 100 positions, a top 10 above 36% is a real concentration figure, not a cosmetic one. In particular, a point of attention is the very high dependence on Boeing, which undoubtedly represents a strong idiosyncratic exposure toward a single issuer.

Peer

Naturally it makes sense to compare it with iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) and Invesco Preferred ETF (PGX). Here it is interesting because PFXF completely excludes the financial sector, while traditional preferred ETFs like PFF and PGX have been described as often dominated by banks and insurance companies, with very high exposure to bank capital.

This positively affects the dividend yield component, and especially post-2022, it has worked in favor of PFXF’s total returns.

Being at long maturity, I also like to consider them as equity-like instruments. In the sense that the underlying trend follows that of U.S. equities, which in a certain sense gives a pleasant underlying predictability, with the difference of having distributions from preferred stocks.

Valuations

In my view, the fund becomes interesting or relatively attractive when long Treasury yields remain high because this keeps the prices of perpetual preferred and hybrid securities low, creating potential for revaluation in the event of a decline in long yields.

The fund can, in fact, work well (afterwards) in contexts of declining long yields, stable or compressing spreads, and economic growth sufficient to limit defaults. Being preferred stocks, therefore equity-like instruments, they become relatively more attractive when the earnings yield of the U.S. market compresses.

My opinion

At this point we have all the necessary information to introduce my thesis. It develops in two directions: monetary on the yield curve side, fundamental on the earnings yield of the S&P 500. First of all, the conflict in Iran has reduced the spread between the 10-year and 2-year yields, because expectations for Fed rate cuts have disappeared together with the increase in inflation expectations, caused by energy inflation. A fairly residual reduction anyway, and generated by a more than proportional increase in the 2-year Treasuries (therefore there is no divergence in this sense, and 10-year Treasuries continue to remain high in terms of relative yields compared to those of the last 20 years).

This, in my view, still makes preferreds interesting: it keeps prices low (below par), and yields high (competitive distributions).

Moving to the second element, the earnings yield of the S&P 500 today is low, equal to 3.49%, and declining (this is because of extreme valuations close to the post-COVID highs of 2021). A situation like this, in my view, makes PFXF interesting, having a distribution of around 6.78%, as we have seen. Resulting in a negative ERP.

Risk

A situation that, however, balances poorly with the other side of the coin. Preferreds are instruments issued by companies; therefore, the demand and supply of these securities will depend a lot on the expected quality of the credit. In this sense, a widening credit spread is not great news for the price performance of this segment (with equity-like oscillations). Following somewhat the pattern of what happened in 2022, PFXF’s holdings have proven to be sensitive, even more so the components of 17-19% high yield and over 50% unrated.

Today I think that the size of the widening in the credit spread will depend on the duration of the conflict in Iran, because it (directly) impacts expectations for energy inflation (therefore expectations for Fed rates). In this, as shared in this coverage of the S&P 500 of mine, historical analysis tends to converge statistically toward a temporary oil shock. This would reduce the weight of the widening of the credit spread, in favor of the distributive component of PFXF.

Conclusion

I therefore expect a very favorable context for distributions, given by long rates still high, but not critically rising as in 2022, and a yield higher than the earnings yield of the S&P 500. At the same time, if the conflict in the Middle East were to continue to widen the size of the credit spread, preferreds, especially the BB and lower components, would suffer a negative pressure on prices, which would rebalance total returns. In this sense, therefore, I think the right rating today is HOLD.

data.title: Armstrong World Industries: Shares Are Right Where They Belong
data.description:
The last few months have frankly been a really difficult time for investors in Armstrong World Industries (AWI). Back in November of last year, I evaluated the company for a potential upgrade from a ‘hold’ to a ‘buy.’ Leading up to that point, the stock had already outperformed the market for months. Revenue and profitability were rising in key areas, driven not only by organic growth, but by acquisitions as well. But at the end of the day, I maintained that the stock was priced at levels that did not make sense from a value investing standpoint. This led me to reaffirm it as a ‘hold’ candidate, which conveys my belief that the stock should perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future.

Since that time, unfortunately, the stock has pulled back, dropping about 13%. Over that same window of time, the S&P 500 is down 1.6%. So clearly, things have not gone well. The good news is that fundamentals remain robust and management continues to focus on growth prospects. But the bad news is that, even with this share price decline, the stock is not priced at levels that I would consider to be attractive. In fact, on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, shares are priced at levels that indicate a fair value for the business at this moment. Because of this, I will maintain my ‘hold’ rating for now, even though management is optimistic about performance when it comes to 2026.

Still not ready for an upgrade

In the event that you are unfamiliar with Armstrong World Industries, that's understandable. In essence, the company operates as a producer of ceiling systems and related offerings. This makes it a very niche play. But it is one that is heavily tied to the construction, repair, and remodeling industries. Recent financial performance for the business has been surprisingly robust considering how weak its end markets have been. Although office vacancy rates have improved recently, they remain elevated compared to historical levels. The transportation and retail sectors are never terribly exciting, and the latter of these looks to suffer from broader economic conditions, especially stemming from tariffs and persistently high-interest rates. All combined, these end markets account for about 50% of the company's revenue.

Despite some of the challenges that the business faces, recent financial performance has been solid. Revenue in the final quarter of 2025, as an example, was $388 million. That was up from the $368 million that the company reported a year earlier. As revenue increased, net profits expanded from $62 million to $66 million. On an adjusted basis, the rise was from $66 million to $70 million. We happened to see operating cash flow pop from $87 million to $110 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a pop from $69 million to $81 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $112 million to $124 million.

When it comes to 2025 in its entirety, Armstrong World Industries exhibited growth across the board. In the chart above, you can see financial performance when it comes to revenue, net profits, adjusted net profits, and cash flows. Clearly, the company is doing quite well at the moment. However, we do need to dig a bit deeper to really understand all that is going on here. Perhaps the best place to start with this would be to tackle its largest operating segment, which is known as Mineral Fiber. Through this unit, the company produces suspended mineral fiber and fiberglass ceiling systems. This might sound like a boring space. However, it truly is fascinating. For instance, included in this segment would be its 50% ownership interest in a joint venture known as WAVE. This particular venture is focused on producing and selling suspension system grid products and other related offerings. The whole goal here of these products is to support drywall sealing systems, structural and walkable grid systems, and more. I don't know about you, but that's intriguing to me.

Year over year for the final quarter, revenue jumped from $238 million to $245 million. Management chalked this up to higher prices and a change in product mix that more than offset an $8 million hit associated with a reduction in volumes. This volume decline is disappointing, to say the least. And according to management, they were caused by indirect impacts of the federal government shutdown and a reduction in home center demand. Again, the housing market has been particularly challenged as of late. Home prices have been dropping for a couple of years now as the surge in pricing that occurred during and after the COVID-19 pandemic necessitated high-interest rates aimed at combating inflationary pressures. Higher interest rates make homes even more unaffordable, leading to lower buying and construction activities.

As revenue for this segment increased, segment profits grew 17.2%, rising from $89 million to $103 million. Management was very clear that some of this improvement came from favorable inventory valuation impacts. However, the business did also see improvements when it came to manufacturing productivity even at a time when input costs were on the rise. Unfortunately, we cannot say the same when it comes to profitability when it comes to the other operating segment, known as Architectural Specialties, but at least revenue for that unit did grow year over year.

For the uninitiated or rusty, Armstrong World Industries’ Architectural Specialties segment focuses on producing and sourcing specialty ceilings, walls, and other architectural applications for the commercial market. These are materials that are typically rated for fire protection, that offer acoustical control, that are comprised of various materials like metal, resin, glass, and wood, and more. According to management, revenue for this segment jumped 11% year over year, climbing from $129.5 million to $143.7 million. This was mostly because of an $8 million benefit from acquisition activities. But there was also some organic growth, as most of its specialty product categories benefited from stronger demand. Unfortunately, overall profitability here did dip from $23 million to $22 million. But that was mostly the result of acquisitions the company made, as well as a rise in manufacturing costs caused by the timing of custom projects. Despite this, for the year as a whole, the $108 million in segment profits generated by the unit exceeded the $82 million reported a year earlier. So in the grand scheme of things, this looks like it was a blip on the radar.

Management believes that, even though market conditions will be mixed this year, with weakness associated with the retail market especially, all other end markets are expected to remain flat at worst and improve to some extent in some scenarios. The transportation sector in particular should get better this year thanks to $15 billion being invested into airports through this year under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act that President Joe Biden signed into law. The overall expectation for the year is for EBITDA to come in at between $600 million and $620 million. Even at the low end, that would be well above the $555 million the company reported a year earlier. And in all likelihood, this would translate to adjusted net profits of around $347 million and to EBITDA of about $467 million. All of this should be based on revenue of between $1.745 billion and $1.785 billion.

Empowered by these estimates, we can see in the chart below how the stock is valued at the moment. Unfortunately, these are multiples that I associate with being in the fair value range. That's true even on a forward basis. Relative to other similar firms, as shown in that same chart, the stock is not exceptional either. Two of the five firms that I stacked it up against happened to be cheaper than it is on a price to earnings and EV-to-EBITDA basis. And when it comes to the price to operating-cash-flow approach, three of the competitors ended up being cheaper.

Even though I am more neutral on the company in the near term, I am actually operationally bullish about it. For starters, management continues to make investments in growth opportunities. Take, if you will, its push into the exterior metal space. In the past, the company focused on interior applications. And that was a market worth an estimated $1.5 billion. But through three separate acquisitions, including its purchase of Parallel Architectural Products, operates as a designer and producer of extruded aluminum products that are used in exterior architectural applications in a deal worth $7.2 million, The firm has expanded its market opportunity to $2.5 billion between interior applications and exterior metal products. That is fantastic, though it will take time for it to play out.

Another thing to be optimistic about is that management has not been afraid to return capital to shareholders. Since 2021, the business has generated around $1.3 billion in adjusted free cash flow. And during that time, it has been very liberal in returning capital to shareholders. In fact, in 2025, the firm had about $307 million of cash that it allocated between capital expenditures, acquisitions, dividends, and share buybacks. $129 million of that went toward buying back 0.8 million shares. Dividends, meanwhile, totaled $55.2 million.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that Armstrong World Industries is not a great prospect at the moment. I do think it's a very good business. And long-term, it should thrive. But based on valuation primarily, the stock is one that investors should be more neutral on as opposed to bullish. For that reason, I am maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate right now.

data.title: SPXT: Ex-Tech S&P 500 ETF Outperforming This Year Has Imperfections, A Hold
data.description:
Today, I would like to present arguments why the ProShares S&P 500 Ex-Technology ETF (SPXT) does not possess a risk/reward profile appealing enough to buy into it. In other words, I do not anticipate it to deliver consistent, durable alpha versus the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).

I remain mostly committed to the view that I expressed in December that a high single-digit gain is possible for the S&P 500 in 2026, even though I am becoming more skeptical about the possibility of two interest rate cuts given triple-digit Brent price. And in part owing to SPXT's weak GARP characteristics, which I will elaborate on below, I do not anticipate it to outmatch IVV this year. A nuance here though is that if investors start pricing in more profound supply chain disruptions and more expensive oil for longer as a consequence of the Iran war, it might deliver a little more comfortable maximum drawdown (historical precedents do exist) than the S&P 500 ETF.

At the same time, even though it has numerous imperfections investors should pay close attention to, there are no critical disadvantages to base a bearish stance upon, neither in terms of factor mix or performance, so to me, the Hold rating is the optimal one to initiate coverage with.

SPXT Strategy And Portfolio

As of March 10, about 33.2% of the S&P 500 ETF's net assets were allocated to the GICS information technology sector. But obviously, we should keep in mind that the S&P 500 has not always been as tech-heavy as it became in the 2010s and especially in the 2020s.

For example, in 1993, the technology sector accounted for hardly imaginable today 5.9%. Then amid the Internet euphoria of the late 1990s and the formation of the dot-com bubble the sector's weight ballooned, reaching 29.2% in 1999. But after the bubble burst and approximately until the Great Recession, technology was unable to return to its prior glory. However, when the economic crisis struck and financials were decimated, the tech sector seized the top position again, sporting 19% weight in 2010. And now, around sixteen years later, its position looks more robust than ever, as financials, communication, and consumer discretionary combined has an almost the same weight in the S&P 500 (as well as the bottom seven sectors, around 33.74%).

Clearly, the ascent of IT to the very top of the stock market and then its entrenchment there has been the underlying force behind the S&P 500's gains, especially during and after the pandemic. Yet this created certain disproportions investors have a lot of reasons to dislike and can find worth avoiding, including apparent expensiveness and high sensitivity to inflation and interest rate outlook. More recently, the AI economy wild card has been added to the equation as investors have been hastily estimating what tech names in general and software players in particular will thrive and what will suffer, which resulted in the S&P 500 ETFs trailing old-economy ETFs like the State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI).

So, as we know from the ProShares website, the core idea of the S&P 500 Ex-Information Technology Index that SPXT tracks is to un-tech (or de-tech, if you please) the S&P 500. This is how this index is described on the S&P Global website:

The S&P 500® Ex-Information Technology is designed to measure the broad U.S. market, excluding members of the GICS® Information Technology sector.

Importantly, SPXT's strategy was changed in September 2018 (the S&P 500 Ex-Information Technology & Telecommunications Index was abandoned), thus, I believe it is reasonable to ignore its performance delivered prior to that.

So, a few questions are worth asking here.

  • Is it truly possible to completely eliminate tech exposure from the S&P 500 by just filtering out the GICS IT stocks?
  • What are the implications of this for the factor mix and do these changes support longer-term outperformance?
  • Was SPXT capable of beating IVV in the past in terms of annualized or risk-adjusted returns or at least delivering more appealing risk metrics (for example, the downside capture ratio)?

Let us answer all these questions one by one. But before, a mild spoiler: this strategy does not work as efficiently as it might seem.

SPXT Portfolio: There Is Still Some Tech Exposure

My answer to the first question is no. At least, just removing the allocation to the GICS IT sector is not enough to achieve that.

As of March 9, there were 432 equities in the SPXT portfolio, with the key holding being Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), with a 5.35% weight. Obviously, 71 S&P 500 stocks from the IT sector were ignored, so in the IVV portfolio, those names that qualified for SPXT have about 66.55% weight, as per my calculations. Additionally, it is worth mentioning that SPXT is either overweight in the 432 S&P 500 constituents or have similar exposure to them compared to IVV (i.e., both ETFs have around 2 bps allocated to Baxter International Inc. (BAX)).

In terms of sectors, SPXT is overweight in all the remaining ten GICS sectors, with emphasis put on financials and communication.

At first glance, the tech issue was eliminated. But we should not forget about the 'expanded tech sector.' Let us look at the iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF (IGM) that tracks the S&P North American Expanded Technology Sector Index, which is

...composed of North American equities in the technology sector and select North American equities from communication services to consumer discretionary sectors.

My calculations show that seven stocks (or six companies) that qualified for IGM account for 13.2% of SPXT's net assets.

Stock Sector Weight in SPXT
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) Communication 4.59%
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) Communication 3.67%
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) Communication 3.63%
Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) Communication 1.07%
Electronic Arts Inc. (EA) Communication 0.12%
Take-Two Interactive Software, Inc. (TTWO) Communication 0.10%
Match Group, Inc. (MTCH) Communication 0.02%

Data from SPXT and Seeking Alpha

Also, some investors might be considering SPXT as they want to cut their exposure to the AI economy theme that since the beginning of the year has been highly volatile given outsized growth premia have been in focus. But the issue is that while SPXT ignores names like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) by design, it still has sizable allocations to such hyperscalers as GOOG and AMZN.

What does these hidden tech or quasi-tech exposures mean for SPXT's returns this year? They make it similarly sensitive to inflation and interest rates as IVV, which means that if investors start pricing in more economic repercussions from the Iran war (which essentially means multiple compression), SPXT will mirror IVV's decline, though its downside capture ratio should be in the 90s and not 100% or higher (there are historical precedents). However, given no exposure to the GICS IT sector (and the consequences of that for the factor mix), I do not anticipate SPXT will outperform IVV this year.

SPXT Factor Story

What does removing the entire sector from the S&P 500 ETF portfolio do to the factor story? A few quite meaningful shifts on nearly all fronts happen.

Value factor

SPXT currently offers exposure to only five out of nine trillion-dollar companies as it ignores Apple Inc. (AAPL), MSFT, NVDA, and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO). As a consequence, its weighted average market cap is 1.78x smaller than IVV's. At the same time, the decision to ignore the GICS IT sector added around 42 bps to its adjusted weighted average earnings yield and meaningfully lowered the Price/Sales and EV/EBITDA ratios, as per my calculations.

Growth

However, removing the IT sector means a lot of compromises must be tolerated on the growth front. For SPXT, no exposure to IT resulted in an around 6% lower weighted average forward revenue growth rate and 6.8% lower forward EPS growth rate, mainly because IVV's figures are significantly impacted by NVDA.

Also, in terms of GARP metrics, SPXT is not offering anything appealing, with the three key indicators at higher levels than IVV's.

Metric SPXT IVV
PEG Ratio 1.816 1.343
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.635 0.577
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.509 1.133

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Quality

SPXT's quality characteristics are weaker than IVV's but not critically so. For instance, its exposure to stocks with a Quant Profitability grade of B- or higher is just 2.61% weaker than IVV's.

Low volatility

An interesting consequence of the removal of IT is that SPXT has a tilt toward the low volatility factor as illustrated by its weighted average 24-month and 60-month beta coefficients. However, this is chiefly because NVDA is absent from the portfolio—the stock has a 2.24 24-month beta.

SPXT Performance

Since the beginning of the year, SPXT has been doing relatively nicely compared to the S&P 500 ETF, which has been dragged down by IT names. For context, the State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) has declined by more than 4%.

However, since September 2018, SPXT's tech-averse approach has not contributed but detracted from its total returns (and risk-adjusted returns as well).

So, the only full calendar year when SPXT outperformed IVV was 2022.

Year Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
October–December 2018 0.15%
2019 -4.37%
2020 -7.95%
2021 -2.40%
2022 3.93%
2023 -10.07%
2024 -5.00%
2025 -2.75%
January–February 2026 3.24%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

So, looking at the metrics for the October 2018–February 2026 period, we see that no IT exposure has taken around 2.88% from SPXT's annualized return.

Nevertheless, what I do like about SPXT is a few of its risk characteristics. It is true that there is nothing particularly compelling about its risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios), yet its maximum drawdown in the 2022 bear market was not that deep compared to IVV's, plus its downside capture was only 93.48%.

What does that mean in the current environment? It is reasonable to assume that SPXT will capture less than 100% of IVV's downside if the Iran war triggers a correction. However, I am skeptical that it will be capable of keeping pace with IVV in a recovery (or in a quick de-escalation scenario with no correction at all). So, essentially, this could be a story similar to a low volatility ETF: smaller downside capture and anemic upside capture together translating into underperformance.

Liquidity And Expenses

It is worth noting that SPXT's AUM of $266.34 million is adequate yet still relatively small, especially assuming SPXT was incepted in 2015. And the net expense ratio of 9 bps is fairly low, which is a minor positive.

Final Thoughts

In sum, I find it unlikely that SPXT will match IVV's return this year and beyond, as no exposure to the GICS IT sector is more of a detractor, not contributor to its performance. So, I believe it is a Hold.

data.title: GenAI Needs Accenture And Q2 Will Show How
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As global managed services giant Accenture (ACN) gets ready to report its Q2 FY26 earnings in the coming week, the company faces the biggest existential crisis in over 10 years for the company—AI.

Accenture’s shares are down 25% this year already as investors question the relevance of the company’s consulting-based business model in the age of AI. Nothing screams pessimism in Accenture’s shares more than the company’s forward revenue multiple, which currently implies a valuation of just 1.6x FY26 revenues.

What investors are irrationally overlooking is that GenAI is ready for its next phase of explosive growth, and it needs the enterprise distribution network of Accenture to achieve those growth milestones.

Heading into Q2 earnings, Accenture has never been this cheap in over 10 years, and I am reiterating my bullish view on Accenture.

Accenture Will Help The Enterprise Implement AI

I have been calling for an AI inflection in Accenture, but my timing has not worked out so far. My conviction surrounding Accenture’s AI inflection is even higher now, while Accenture’s shares have gotten further cheaper since my post in December of last year.

So far in Q1 this year, investors witnessed the volatile pessimism in the entire software sector due to the strong perception that AI would replace software workflows, leading to the SaaSpocalypse of software. Amidst this rout of software, Accenture’s shares were also weighed heavily by the volatility playing out through Q1, because markets feared AI rendering Accenture’s managed services business as valueless.

In my opinion, there is a case for investors to be fearful about the impact that AI would have on Accenture. But for markets to have that much fear in Accenture that the shares are now priced at just 1.6x FY26 revenues seems unreasonable to me.

The last known estimates from Wall St. analysts project Accenture to be growing its revenue by 6%. That actually is more optimistic than the 2-5% revenue growth projections issued by management in December. Moreover, Q1 FY26 new bookings have already started to inflect and have grown 11.7% in Q1 FY26 to $20.9B.

GenAI is becoming an emerging area for Accenture as more enterprises, like telcos and consumer product companies, look towards implementing GenAI in the enterprise. In the Q1 quarter in December, management revealed that GenAI bookings account for ~10.5% of new bookings, growing at a pace of 2x. And these numbers were all reported for the Q1 quarter.

Since then, the last 3 months have shown how frontier AI labs are quietly striking deep partnerships with Accenture to push for AI to be implemented among the company’s enterprise market.

It started with Anthropic and Accenture partnering “to help enterprises move from AI pilots to full-scale deployment" in December.

In February this year, OpenAI expanded its “push into the enterprise market" by launching the Frontier Alliance with Accenture and 3 other consulting peers. Per the T&C of the Frontier Alliance, OpenAI’s (OPENAI) specialized implementation engineers would work with companies like Accenture on implementation projects to “integrate AI agents into core business processes such as software development, sales, and customer support.”

Two weeks ago, Mistral AI announced their own alliance with Accenture, which would help the latter’s enterprise clients deploy AI at scale.

This is coming at a time when each of these frontier AI labs faces their own pressures in continuing to deliver strong growth. Both Anthropic and OpenAI aim to nearly triple their respective top lines this year, with Anthropic looking to score ~$26B in CY26 revenues while OpenAI plans to grow its revenues to $30B this year. The pressure to grow quickly has made every frontier AI lab look at faster deployment of AI in the enterprise, and Accenture suddenly sits in an advantageous position, in my view, based on its deep relationship with the enterprise.

The strong pickup in new bookings growth in Accenture’s previous quarter was just a small hint of what's to come this year for Accenture, and I suspect that these partnerships with AI frontier labs will start to materialize into actual top-line revenues for Accenture starting in the Q2 ER, which Accenture reports this week.

For investors, there are 2 things they would need to watch in the Q2 ER. First are hints of sustained acceleration in the new bookings, especially if GenAI is accounting for a larger share of new bookings, in excess of the 10.5% of the new bookings metric I noted earlier. The second would be how Q2 gross margins show up for Accenture. If business is really picking up due to increased AI implementation projects, the unit economics for Accenture will pick up, pushing gross margin expansion, which has so far stayed range-bound between 31 and 33%.

Markets think Accenture won't deliver any of the two growth factors I mentioned in the previous paragraph, as seen in the valuation multiples.

Accenture’s Valuation Shows Elevated Fears, Or Opportunities?

As I mentioned earlier, Accenture’s shares trade at just 1.6x FY26 sales, expected to grow 6% this year, ahead of management’s 2-5% revenue growth projections.

During FY22-FY25, Accenture’s top line grew at ~4% CAGR while the shares traded at 2.8-3.2x forward sales. Therefore, for the company to now trade at 1.6x forward sales versus expectations of 6% forward revenue growth seems unreasonable.

I expect Accenture to be a winner this year compared to the vulnerable positions the stock was in last year and in this quarter.

Risks To My Thesis

There is a strong possibility that Accenture might not raise its revenue forecasts, even if AI begins to play a larger role in the company’s growth outlook. In such cases, Accenture’s shares might continue to face pressure until management is able to change the perception of the outlook. Alternatively, if there is a management transition, expect Accenture’s shares to see more upside.

What investors are looking for in Q2 are as follows:

  • Q2 FY26E: $2.84 EPS (flat) on revenues worth $17.83B (up 7%)
  • Q3 FY26E: $3.73 EPS (up 7%) on revenues worth $18.71B (up 5.5%)
  • FY26E: $13.84 EPS (up 7%) on revenues worth $74B (up 6%)

Takeaway

I expect AI to finally start playing a major role in Accenture’s consulting business and am looking forward to the company’s Q2 ER which should start to show stronger hints of AI growth in the new bookings.

I am reiterating my bullish view on Accenture ahead of Q2 ER this week.
